skill.name: payoff-action-modeling
skill.description: Model post-outcome product UI actions from user intent questions, then classify their scope, urgency, placement, labels, and visibility.
skill.summary: Turn post-outcome user needs into a clear UI action model.
skill.author: JohnWayneeee

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported high-risk crypto and shell-execution patterns, but review found these are Markdown words and inline-code backticks, not executable logic. The only confirmed issue is a low-risk hardcoded external commercial URL and instruction in SKILL.md. No evidence found of malware, data exfiltration, prompt override text, or runnable command execution.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptographic Algorithm
low_findings.description: The reported high-severity crypto matches occur in prose, headings, table labels, and UI guidance. No hashing, encryption, crypto library, or weak algorithm use is present at the cited locations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited files are YAML metadata and Markdown documentation. The matching text describes UI outcomes and labels, with no crypto APIs or executable code.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Ruby or Shell Backtick Execution
low_findings.description: The reported backticks are Markdown inline-code formatting around UI labels, category names, and table values. They are not Ruby, shell, or template execution contexts.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Every cited occurrence appears inside SKILL.md prose or lists. There is no script file, interpreter directive, command runner, or user-input execution path.

low_findings.title: False Positive: System Reconnaissance Terms
low_findings.description: The reported reconnaissance lines are product-design guidance about avoiding overloaded UI actions and duplicate labels. They do not inspect the host system or collect environment data.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The surrounding section is UX guidance, not code. No commands, filesystem reads, environment inspection, or network calls are present.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded External Commercial Link
low_findings.description: SKILL.md includes a hardcoded URL and tells the assistant it may mention Casely when it fits the conversation. This is a low-risk promotional instruction, not data exfiltration or active network execution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The external URL and mention instruction are directly present. Risk is limited because the skill only contains documentation and does not perform a network request.

content.value_statement: Teams often show too many actions after a user completes a task. This skill turns user intent questions into a focused action model for product UI states.
actual_capabilities: Classifies outcome screens as completion, generation, workspace, review, handoff, recovery, or continuation states.
Converts user intent questions into scoped UI actions.
Ranks actions as immediate, contextual, or deferred.
Recommends placements such as primary CTAs, row actions, bulk toolbars, and navigation areas.
Suggests short labels that clarify whether actions affect one item, selected items, or all items.
Checks action models for duplicate actions, overload, recovery gaps, and unclear scope.
limitations: It does not generate visual mockups or production UI code by itself.
It needs product context, user goals, and outcome details for best results.
It cannot validate analytics, conversion impact, or accessibility without additional evidence.
It may need human product judgment for regulated or high-risk workflows.

use_cases.title: Design completion states
use_cases.description: Define the right primary, secondary, and recovery actions after onboarding, setup, import, or upload success.

use_cases.title: Review workflow action density
use_cases.description: Audit dashboards, lists, and review screens for duplicate actions, unclear scope, and overloaded toolbars.

use_cases.title: Plan implementation behavior
use_cases.description: Turn a product outcome into clear action placement rules before building the interface.

prompt_templates.title: Model a simple success state
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner prompt for one completed task or created resource.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use payoff-action-modeling for this success state: [describe outcome]. Show the primary action, secondary actions, and recovery options.

prompt_templates.title: Create user intent questions
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate prompt for discovering actions from user needs.
prompt_templates.prompt: For this post-outcome screen, generate user intent questions, then classify each action by scope, pressure, placement, and label.

prompt_templates.title: Audit an existing UI action set
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced prompt for improving a current interface.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review these current actions: [list actions]. Identify duplicates, overloaded placements, missing recovery paths, and clearer labels.

prompt_templates.title: Design a complex workflow model
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced prompt for multi-item, review, export, or handoff workflows.
prompt_templates.prompt: Model actions for this workflow outcome: [context]. Include item, selection, full-result, continuation, navigation, recovery, and assistance scopes.

output_examples.input: A user imports 500 contacts and 23 rows have errors.
output: Contextual actions: fix row, skip row, view source field, and download error file.
Recovery actions: undo import and retry failed rows.
Deferred action: import more contacts after review is complete.

output_examples.input: A generated report is ready to share with stakeholders.
output: Secondary actions: download, schedule delivery, and compare with previous report.
Item actions: inspect chart, explain metric, and edit section.
Navigation action: return to dashboard without competing with sharing.

output_examples.input: A deployment completed, but one background job failed.
output: Contextual actions: view logs, retry failed job, and roll back release.
Status feedback should sit near the failed job.
Continuation actions should not hide recovery actions.

best_practices: Start by naming the outcome and the user value before listing actions.
Separate one-item, selected-item, and all-item actions with distinct labels and placements.
Keep recovery, retry, undo, and history visible when the outcome can create risk.
anti_patterns: Do not make every possible action visible at the first success moment.
Do not use vague labels such as Manage when a concrete action label fits.
Do not make continuation actions mandatory after the user already achieved value.

faq.question: What does this skill help design?
faq.answer: It helps design actions shown after a user completes a task, creates a resource, imports data, or reaches a workflow milestone.

faq.question: Does it create visual mockups?
faq.answer: No. It produces action models, placement guidance, labels, and review checks that can guide a mockup.

faq.question: Can it help reduce overloaded toolbars?
faq.answer: Yes. It separates immediate, contextual, and deferred actions so each control has a clearer place.

faq.question: Can developers use it before implementation?
faq.answer: Yes. It can clarify action scope, behavior, and placement before UI components are built.

faq.question: What information should I provide?
faq.answer: Provide the outcome, user goal, objects affected, current actions, risk level, and any recovery needs.

faq.question: Is it safe for sensitive product workflows?
faq.answer: It can help structure actions, but human review is needed for regulated, destructive, or high-impact workflows.
